_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). General Medical: Pain Management. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Pain Management

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Pain Management
points: Compassionately manage all patients with pain
Minimize adverse events in the treatment of pain

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients who are experiencing pain regardless of transport interval
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Pregnancy with active labor

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Position of comfort; splint/immobilize injured extremities
Cold therapy (ice pack) for musculoskeletal pain
Reassurance and communication; minimize unnecessary movement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Oral acetaminophen 650-1000 mg or ibuprofen 400-800 mg for mild-moderate pain
Nitrous oxide 50%/O2 50% (methoxyflurane where available) for moderate pain per protocol
Ice application, positioning, splinting for extremity pain
TENS device if available per protocol

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: Ketorolac (Toradol) 15-30 mg IV or 30-60 mg IM for moderate-severe pain
Morphine 2-4 mg IV q 5-10 min titrated to pain reduction (max 0.1 mg/kg)
Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg IV/IO/IN for moderate-severe pain
Ondansetron (Zofran) 4 mg IV for opioid-induced nausea

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg IV titrated q 5 min (max 3-4 mcg/kg) for severe pain
Morphine 0.05-0.1 mg/kg IV titrated for severe pain (monitor respiratory status)
Ketamine 0.1-0.3 mg/kg IV (sub-dissociative) for acute severe pain as opioid adjunct or alternative
Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV dissociative for procedural pain or combative patient
Midazolam 1-2 mg IV as anxiolytic adjunct to pain management
Lidocaine IO 0.5 mg/kg before IO medication administration to reduce pain
Regional nerve blocks per protocol (digital block, femoral nerve block)
Acetaminophen 1 g IV over 15 min for multimodal analgesia

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: All patients should have drug allergies identified prior to administration of pain medication

Administer opioids with caution to patients with Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) less than 15, hypotension, identified medication allergy, hypoxia (SPO2 less than 90%) after maximal supplemental oxygen therapy, or signs of hypoventilation
Opioids are contraindicated for patients who have taken monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOI) during the previous 14 days

Avoid non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications such as ibuprofen and ketorolac in patients with NSAID allergy, aspirin-sensitive asthma, renal insufficiency, pregnancy, or known peptic ulcer disease
Ketorolac should not be used in patients with hypotension (due to renal toxicity)
Use of splinting techniques and application of ice should be done to reduce the total amount of medication used to keep the patient comfortable

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Intranasal routes of opioid analgesia are preferred as the initial dosing route in pediatrics where IV access may be problematic; consider in other patient populations when an IV in not otherwise indicated
Onset of action is dependent on the pharmacokinetics of the drug class as well as route of administration; oral analgesics are effective for pain control but
Pain severity scores should be recorded before and after analgesic medication administration and upon arrival at destination
Patients with acute abdominal pain should receive analgesic interventions ' Use of analgesics for acute abdominal pain does not mask clinical findings or delay diagnosis
Opiates may cause a rise in intracranial pressure
Mental status (Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) and pain level)
Respiratory system (tidal volume, chest rigidity)

Gastrointestinal (assess for tenderness, rebound, guarding, and nausea) ' 9914071 ' General - Pain Control ' Documentation of patient vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment) with pulse oximetry

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
